stem: A city's transportation department surveyed 250 randomly selected commuters at a downtown train station between 7:00 AM and 9:00 AM on a Tuesday. Of those surveyed, 68% said they support a proposed increase in public transit funding. The city has 180,000 total commuters. Based on this survey, which of the following statements is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Approximately 122,400 commuters in the city support the funding increase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The survey provides a reliable estimate only for commuters who use the downtown train station during morning rush hour.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sample size is too small to make any valid conclusions about commuter opinions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the sample was randomly selected, the 68% figure accurately represents all commuters in the city.

explanation: While the calculation $0.68 \times 180,000 = 122,400$ is mathematically correct, this inference is not statistically valid. The survey only sampled commuters at one downtown train station during morning rush hour on a weekday. This group may have different opinions than commuters who use other stations, travel at different times, or use other forms of transportation like buses or cars. The sample was randomly selected from people at that specific location and time, but this does not make it representative of all 180,000 commuters in the city. The survey results can only be reliably generalized to the population it actually sampled from: commuters using that particular train station during those specific hours.

distractorRationale.A: This answer performs the correct arithmetic ($0.68 \times 180,000 = 122,400$) but fails to recognize that mathematical accuracy does not equal statistical validity. Students who choose this confuse calculating a proportion with validly generalizing from a non-representative sample.
distractorRationale.B: CORRECT: Recognizes that the sampling method limits generalization. The sample was only drawn from one specific subset of commuters (downtown train station, morning rush hour), so conclusions can only apply to that group.
distractorRationale.C: This represents the misconception that sample size alone determines validity. A sample of 250 from 180,000 (about 0.14%) is actually reasonable for statistical purposes—the issue is not size but representativeness of the sampling method.
distractorRationale.D: This confuses random selection within a location with random selection from the entire population. While individuals at the station were randomly chosen, the decision to survey only at that one location and time introduces sampling bias that prevents generalization to all city commuters.
